CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The Suffers in Dublin are playing their little imitation
British game, and they have got some keenly desired
notoriety by window smashing. So far as the silly
Suffers in this country are concerned they are the last

~ persons that we would be inclined to add to the motley
. - brigade of voters. Irish women are merely laughing
at them, yet these Suffers, in the same document in
which they mention.the word ‘‘ logically,” claim to
speak for Irish women. Of course, Irish women re-
udiate them. They go out and break windows. How
uld they like it if some people went out and kid-
apped their Skefiy and cut off his whiskers? It
ppears. that some silly Suffer unanimous resolutions
rere sent to all Irish- Members, but not one of the
ter took the trouble to acknowledge them. Ignor-
the silly Suffers is evidently the unpardonable sin.
‘ortunately for the silly Suffers—and perhaps for our
ows too—they sometimes como in so handy for us
copy "’ that we occasionally refer to them, and to
1 he window smashers and their accom-

plices regard themselves as representing Irish women,
and they go break windows in the name of Irish woman!
There is sense for you! Irish women repudiate these
people, and their attitude of amused contempt for them
has probably developed into strong disgust since the
window-smashing escapade.  The manifesto of the
self-styled *“ Irish Women’s Franchise League '’ winds
up with a pleasant threat:—*‘ It (breaking G.P.O. and
other windows) is but an earnest of what is in store
if an Irish Constitution is set up without including
qualified woman-voters.”” -Citizens of Dublin, put up
your shutters!

The Skeffy gang appears to have had an .unusually
lively time of it last Saturday in the Pheenix Park.
Apparently the window-breaking of the Skeffy gang
induced an extra large audience to the Suffer pitch in
the Pheenix on Saturday. It appears by all accounts
to have been a humorously hostile audience. Skeffy,
the only male, apparently, of the gang present, came
in for some banter and hustling from the crowd. There
were cries of ‘‘ cut his whiskers off *’; ““ put a breeches
on him.”” This latter remark was rather unintelligible,
for his everlasting breeches is as characteristic of Skeffy
as are his whiskers. More to the point was the remark :
‘“ The breeches should be taken off him and a skirt put
on.””  Certainly if the Skeffy gang have destroyed
Government property, at least they are supplying, free,
gratis, and for nothing, plenty of what the Dublin
jackeen calls ‘‘ gas.” No doubt about it there was
““ great gas ”’ in the Pheenix on Saturday evening, and
all to be enjoyed without charge for admission too.

In view of the Skeffy gang presuming to speak for
Irish women, it is not much use arguing with them.
A Mrs. Palmer declared that Home Rule would not
be acceptable to the Irish people unless there was a
clause in it giving votes for women. We note that the
declaration was received with laughter, and a ‘‘ voice "
said: ““ You are in a hobble now.”” Mus. Skeffington
said, according to one Press report:—

She wondered did the Irish Party realise that if
they had the women of this country with them they
could make a very good thing of Home Rule, but
if they had not the women with them the Home
Rule Bill might go through, but it would be a
horrible fiasco.

That is the sort of thing that helps to make the Skeffy
gang ridiculous. Of course, the women of Nationalist
Ireland are with Home Rule : the women of Ireland are
against the Skeffy gang and their posturings and
window-smashing.  Mrs. Skeffington has challenged
Mr. Asquith to address a meeting in the Pheenix.  Why
merely to address a meeting?  As, according to the
Skeffy gang, men and women are on an equality in their
capacities as citizens, why not challenge him to the Nine
Acres and give him, as is, we believe, customary in
these cases, the choice of weapons?  If Murphy’s
trams have the interests of their shareholders at heart
they should plaster their cars on the Pheenix Park
route on the days of these public entertainments with
an announcement: ‘‘ The Skeffy Gang in the Park to-
day.” Is it quite fair to the various ofher entertainers’
of Dublin that the Skeffy gang should be giving all this
amusement free of charge? Does it not border on.what
is called *‘ sweated ’:labour? Unless Skeffy charges
for admission the other entertainers should threaten a
strike and insist on Skeffy not underselling in the
amusement market. 3

-

Taking them: all' round the tramway conductors of
Dublin are a: very decent and obliging lot of*fellows,
and, considering. the hard work they have to do for
rather small pay, we often wonder.-that they are gener-
.lly in such good humour. There are exceptions, of
course. Recently a passenger had a few words with
a conductor on the Dalkey line, whose face he did not
Temember having seen before. Perhaps he was a raw
member of the staff. He was rather a youngster.
The fare from Nassau Street to Elgin Road is 1d. In
accordance with a rather prevalent custom the pas-
senger waited for a tram at the point where the trams
are about to turn into Nassau Street, boarded one and
paid his penny as he and others have done scores of
times before. ~What was his surprise when the youth-
ful conductor informed him at Haddington Road that
his:fare had gone out as the gas goes out in a penny-
in-the-slot metre.  The passenger told him he had
got in at Nassau Street and the fare ended at Elgin
Road; but the chap contended that the passenger got
in too soon before the tram stopped!  What will
Murphy’s trams want to do next? 'They ought to
put & chalk mark at the various points so that people
may avoid boarding a tram, one or two feet from the
mark! At most the passenger could scarcely have
been ten yards from Nassau Street when he boarded
the tram, and according to the young man he was to
be regarded as having got in at Nelson Pillar within
the meaning of the Tramway regulations! Of course,
he refused to pay him an extra penny to carry him to
Blgin Road, and gave the chap his name and address.
If the Tramway Co. take our advice they will in their
own interests exasperate the Dublin public as little as
they can help.. In this particular case the
officious conductor had scarcely a technical
quibble to stand wupon, for the trams when
coming the other way do not stop for Nassau
Street end until they turn the cormer into the
lower part of Grafton Street, so that if the conductor
was tight, old ladies travelling on a penny ticket from
Elgin Road should jump out before the tram stopped,
or else pay an extra penny when it did stop!

A TRINITY COLLEGE SCENE.

Scene—A Hall in Trinity College.
(Enter Carson and Jim Crow in armour.)

Carson.—I tell thee, Jim,
Thqu mighty Ajax of the crow brigade,
The Greeks within this home of ancient lore
‘Want waking up.

Jim.—They’re mouldy grown.
The fire of valour in their blood doth wane.
Yet I do think Mahaffy still would break
A lance against a Papist, or great Tony,
In plated armour on a war horse couched,
Would on a Home Rule phalanx thunder down
Like an avenging Hector of the ‘‘ saved.”
But Culverwell on war’s terrific path
Would never flash the sword.

Carson.-——Hang him,
The cold and cautious counsellor of care
And strategy unwarlike. The fellow shows
A bad example to the fogey’s here
In bringing economic sense to bear
Upon the Home Rule menace. Why don’t he talk
Of bloody wars.
Jim.—His craven wisdom may deflect them all
From battle’s gory purpose.
Carson.—His sane advice
Tull many of the ““ saved '’ may take to heart,
And never more make donkeys of themselves
By threats of blood and battle.
The gory feeling to the boiling point,
Or else the game is lost. Buck up, Jim Crow,
And on your face put on the fiercest look
Of deadly war.
Jim.—How do I look now.?
Carson.—Most grim and dangerous.
Jim.—Ha! that’s well.
A thousand hearts are great within my bosom,
And I do feel that in a battle charge
I could unhorse a doughty foe as big
As Tony Traill.
Carson.—Here comes that vile Uly.
Professor Culverwell.
(Enter Culverwell.)
Jim.—Thou man of peace,
Behold two valiant champions of the caws,
And let thy heart rejoice.
Culver.—I see before me
Two men of law, who’re long beyond their prime,
And badly wanting jobs.
Carson.—Dull-witted man,
Do you not see in us two peerless chiefs
To lead the Union legions to a field
Of glorious victory ?
Jim.—Where are your eyes?
That in Jim Campbell decked in war array,
A mighty viking of the loyal north
Cannot detect.
Culver.—I pray you,
To put this Guy Fawkes masquerade away
And fight Home Rule upon the proper field
As men of sense, who have an axe to grind
At some no distant date. It grieves me sore
To see such able placehunters behave
Like penny showmen at a village fair,
Who set the rustic clodhoppers agape
With dull delight and wonder.
Carson.—Stop, stop, or else I'll draw
My sword against thee.
Jim.—Rash man, forbear,
Or else Jim Crow against thy
‘Will couch his lance.
Culver.—Come on; I fear you not,
You parlour fighters.
(Carson and Crow charge upon Culverwell, and are
driven back. Confusion—Curtain).

natural and national opposition that the Hairy Faivies

sums paid in yearly salaries by this ‘ liberal * Univer-

two idolaters receive salaries, the Professor of Scholastic

Philosophy and the Librarian! They divide the

It is a rather good thing for Home Rule that the anti-
Home Rule Party is composed of such a collection of
variegated botches.  Mr. Bonar Law appears to be
quite a success from our point of view, for he does not
appear to be much good. Old Carson of the shake
hands is the right man in the right place—for Home
Rule; a fellow named Garvin—a renegade Nationalist
—is rather a good man for us too. The London corres-
pondent of " the *‘ Irish Times ' holds Garvin up to
ridicule—a good thing for us., He rather rashly refers

. to Garvin’s *‘ zeal of a convert "’ : should he not have
gone the whole hog and written pervert? This Garvin
* josser "—as our friend ‘‘ John Bull Junr.”’ might
call him—suggests that the Unionist Party should
eventually stop all business in the House of Commons
and drown every Ministerial speaker by the steady cry
of ““ dissolve, dissolve.”” The *‘ Irish Times '’ London
correspondent asks: ‘ Did any responsible journalist
ever put forward such a ridiculous idea?’’ We hope
that the man Garvin will not be intimidated by the
attempted ridicule of the London correspondent of
““ Alf Fox.” Go on, Garvin. Rats, Rats!

observe that-several men were co-opted on the com-
mittee. I understand that the object aimed at is to
make the homes of the people bright and happy, but
how they are going to accomplish this I am unable to
say. I attended the initial meeting at the school-house,
and indeed, as meetings go, it was a highly respectable
and representative gathering, and the sentiments ex-
pressed were very worthy ones, It is scarcely neces-
sary to mention that the principal speaker was an .
organiser, who very likely is not working for nothing,
but where she came from, or who sent her I do not
know. She said a good deal about rearing children,
and other ‘things, but I fear the general run of the
people cannot have much confidence in her, for as she is
a spinster she cannot have much knowledge of children.
The people as a rule do not take kindly to organisations
of this kind, for many reasons, and the principal one I
think is, that they are inclined to resent interference
with their domestic life, and they also doubt the ability
to help, and often the genuineness of the desire to do so.
Indeed, as to ability, in this case I have grave doubts;
for, as regards what I know of the committee, a careful
mother would not trust one of them to hold her baby .
during the time it would take her to wine a
chair  for the visitor. A good many of them
are spinsters and bachelors of ripe age. Then
as to the sincerity—we all know the amount
of “snobbery which prevails in our small towns;
an eminent lawyer once described it, as fourpenny nails
looking down on pig-iron—and when you find the
names of people associated with this movement, who
would consider themselves soiled if they accidentally
rubbed against those whom they believe to be their
inferiors, you are inclined to doubt the likelihood of such
persons going into the homes of their poorer neighbours
to suggest improvements or taking a turn at minding
the baby if intrusted with it. I believe myself that
this latest addition will have the shortest life of our
many associations, but perhaps the main object is
attained with the greater number when a public meet-

HERE is really no limit in these progressive days

to the achievements of science.  Marvel suc-

ceeds marvel. The very latest discovery of which news
has come to hand is, perhaps, the most astounding of
all.  Should it prove feasible to realise the hopes of the
ingenious inventor of this newest miracle it is not too
much to say that this latest development of scientific
study will revolutionise the world. It will bring about
a change far-reaching and disturbing.  Ireland, too,
will feel its effects no less than any other portion of
the globe.  That it will prove a boon to all save a
limited and undeserving class cannot be denied. Of
course, it remains to be seen whether this new scien-
tific wonder will stand the test of time, and whether
it will be able to accomplish all that is claimed for it.
Needless to say, sceptics have already hinted at the
possibility of failure.
This latest addition to sci
genius of Signor Meritonli. b is gratifying to note
that he is in some degree an Irishman. He was born
in America of Italian and English parents, but his
grand-aunt married an Irish-American. He is proud
of his connection with Ireland, and has quite an Irish
smile.  For many years Signor Meritonli has been
engaged on investigations into the possibility of sub-
stituting a scheme of Wireless Jobbery for the com-
plicated system at present in vogue. It was his idea
that a simple method could be devised, by means of
which it would be possible, when jobs were required to
be filled, to attract the proper objects to posts without
the use of wires. The scheme seems, it must be
allowed, Utopian and almost absurd. At the first blush,
indeed, it looks impossible of realisation, but Signor
Meritonli is reported to be confident that he has dis-
covered a method by which it can be carried out. The
details, we understand, are not fully perfected as yet,
and, of course, it would not be possible in a necessarily

A few words, however, on the nature of this wonderful
. discovery may not be out of place.

7 As the name of the new invention implies, the pith
¥ ' of this discovery lies in the abolition of wires. The
highly complicated and nerve-trying system of wire-
pulling—to use the technical term—in the matter of
Jobs, which is now indispensable, is to be completely
done away with. ~Furthermore, under the new system
all underground work and agencies will be put an end
to.  This obviously, if it can be done, must have an
immensely beneficial effect on the physical and even
moral welfare of humanity. At present the time and
labour expended in subterranean channels manipulat-
ing *‘ live ’ wires has unquestionably a highly detri-
mental affect on those engaged in this anxious work.
Moreover, it is agreed on all hands that it does not
result in that efficiency which is so highly prized in
these strenuous days. By conducting operations
under the new system above ground in the open air, it
is hoped to achieve the maximum of efficiency with
the minimum of peril to the interests of the commu-
nity.

Signor Meritonli proposes in the first place to set up
posts boldly in public view. At all stations where
such posts are deemed necessary they will be dis-
played above ground, and full particulars as to their
purpose and whereabouts will be duly announced
through the medium of the public Press. It is hoped
in this way to obviate all the present cumbersome
tapping, touching, and feeling which proves so irksome
and wearying to all concerned. Not alone will wires
cnly be dispensed with, practically the whole appara-
tus now in use will go by the board. The metal discs
and currency, now so frequently employed, will no
longer be requisite.  The rapidly-circulating Huid so
indispensable, and frequently so dangerous, without
which Jobbery cannot at present be worked, will be
abandoned. Semaphoring, or the making of Signs,
which plays so important a part under the present
system, will also no longer have any utility. The re-
liance on ‘* influence,”’ nepotic and otherwise, will
cease. A perusal of this list of reforms will show how
complete a revolution Signor Meritonli hopes to accom-
plish.

§ The great principle underlying the new plan is the
proper utilisation of light. — Hitherto this important
r. element has been neglected. The post in future is to be
: set up in public, the magnetic attraction of gold or silver
(as to the indispensable nature of which all scientists
are agreed) is to be properly displayed, the objects will
respond, and, as a result of a scheme of elal;orat-e and
searching ** tests ”’ the most fitting only will become
attached to the post. The others will- return whence
they came, or become cast down and scattered in all
directions.  As already stated it is too early yet to
give full details of the precise mebhods by which the
new invention will operate, but enough has been _smd
to convey to the dullest an outline of the great project.

The news of the discovery has naturally produced
something like a sensation in all the great Jobbing
centres.  Already a project is on foot to make the in-

AN IRISH-IRELAND COLLEGE.

HE traveller along the old road which stretches
between Passage and Rochestown, about four or

five miles from the city of Cork, cannot fail to have
his attention arrested by the singular beauty of the
countryside which surrounds the College of the
Capuchin Fathers.  The hills all round are thickly
covered with woods, while the green of the fields and
the rich red of the tillage proclaim the fertility of the
soil. The surmounting of the hill which lies behind
the college reveals a scene of surpassing splendour,
the waters of Lough Mahon stretch away on all sides
reflecting the sylvan glories of Glanmire and Little
Island. The harshness of the city is entirely softened
by the distance which intervenes between it, and one
perceives it only as a blue, misty dwelling-place tucked
away between the hills and intersected by the glitter-
ing waters of the Lee. Looking westward at sunset on
a summer’s evening one can see the expanse of the
Lough threading its way between the undulating
woods to mingle its waters with those of the Tramore
at Douglas. ~ At such -an hour the whole resembles

the keen observer still greater wonders are exposed;

It was on a Sunday evening that I paid it my first
visit, and at a time when the swelling strains of the
chapel organ echoed over the placid waters of the little
lake which lies in front of the building, so appropriate
was the setting that one’s thoughts immediately went
back to pillaged shrines which cover our land, and you
thought of what Ireland must have been in the days of
her saints and scholars.
. One would little think that this noble pile of build-
ings was a factory, and yet a factory it is. For within
the forces of Irish Ireland are being hammered and
welded into shape. It is doubtful if any of the Irish
Colleges which have been founded by the Gaelic
League are doing more work for the regeneration of
Ireland than the Franciscans ave doing at their college
at Rochestown. The spirit of Irish Ireland pervades
everywhere from the mosaic pltamce at the entrance
to the portrait of Canon O’Leary which meets the eye
in the study hall, and the genial Father who conducted
me through the building told me that Canon O’Leary
was the guiding spirit of the school. No opportunity
is lost to instil the principles of Irish Ireland into the
minds of the boys, and the fruitful results of their
endeavours are plain even to the most inobservant.
The lads here can sing, and dance, and play, and their
songs, dances and games are all Irish.  If they sing
for you it is in Irish, or else in the English of Davis
and the Young Irelanders; they dance the reel and jig
with a skill and intricacy that bewilders and enchants
the uninitiated. The sight and sound of a Rugby ball
is as foreign to Rochestown as the camaén is to colleges
nearer home. - The hills around Rochestown know
well the echo of the ash as it has oftimes resounded in
hard-fought combat.

One cannot but reflect on details such as these.
Which is the more admirable type, the precocious
city-bred boy with his cricket-bat, his music-hall
songs, and his waltzes and polkas, or the type which
colleges like Rochestown turn out with the games,
songs and dances of the Gael? T think it would not be
hard to designate which should prove the more desir-
able citizen of Ireland. If all the colleges of Treland
had the same spirit of Irish ‘Ireland guiding them we
should not have the heterogeneous Ireland of to-day,

* and, -perhaps, it is not too much to expect that in time

such will be the case.

When one recollects the despair of the great-
hearted Prelate of Cashel he cannot help feeling that
we have at least advanced a few steps on the onward
path. The existence of such a college as that at
Rochestown justifies such a belief, and the remem-
brance of the silent machinery here spinning and
weaving the regeneration of Ireland sends a feeling of
case and calm through the mind as to the future of
Treland. It is a Significant fact that in conjunction
with the Irish Ireland colleges we have the spectacle

IRISH SCHOOLS AND IRISH NEEDS.

YHE addresses and discussions at the Cork
Technical Congress last week were of more
general interest and value than the proceedings of
many previous congresses. To an ordinary outsider
with any regard for National development they had, I
think, an unusual importance.

Irish public opinion had long realised that the
Intermediate education system, though it perhaps pre-
pares a pupil very fairly for following up the University
course, does not supply a suitable training for the
careers in the reach of the great majority of its
students who seek a means of livelihood immediately
on leaving the Intermediate school—especially that it
fits them least of all for industrial, commercial, or
farming life. But definite schemes for providing the
proper training were seldom so clearly put forward as
by Dr. Starkie and Mr. Gill—at least not in a way
or on an occasion to strike the public attention. There
must have been many who could never see any need
or public advantage to be expected from Dr. Starkie’s
favourite scheme of Higher National Schools, till his
fine, forceful address showed what is to be said for it.
I cannot yet understand why, in addition to the tech-
nical schools and classes, the numerous cheap
Intermediate schools of the country and the system of
exhibitions, with the addition of the Secondary School
Scholarship scheme which is being framed, will not
provide opportunities enough for the cleverer boys of
the National schools to follow up a suitable course if
their school programmes were only modified, as Mr.
Gill explained, to meet the wants of the larger number.

Mr, Gill's alternative, which it is hopeful to learn is
about to be put in practice to some extent, seems
much simpler, and more veadily and quickly intro-
duced and as likely to effect the same end without any
of its cost. It is that the Intermediate Board, by use
of the power it has to shape the programme of the
schools which compete for its examinations, should
get them to introduce courses of veally suitable train-
ing for farming and industrial and commercial life, to-

of the 62 Conferences of the ancient diocese attended,
together with a great gathering of other members and
the general public. The occasion was a rallying of the
forces, for the purpose of reviewing the work done by
the powerful society.  Pithy, practical papers on the
topics of the day, such as National Insurance, Boys’
H01nes, Medi@val and Modern Socialism, and Distribu-
tion of Catholic Literature were read by the members,
and elicited ‘hearty applause, as they showed that the
subjects treated of were thoroughly studied. The
Bishop, in expressing his earnest commendation, drew
attention to the fact that proceedings like those of the
Congress were the greatest factors for the education of
Catholics, and at no time in the history of our country
was this aid so much needed. In eloquent and forceful
words he pointed out that German Catholics sixty years
ago, under the guidance of Bishop Kelteler, and later
Windthorst, formed themselves into the mighty Centre
Party in Parliament and the annual Catholic Con-
gresses, which deserve so well of religion. The

June 22, 1912.

Catholics of Belgium have followed the example of their
German  co-religionists, and have vanquished the
enemies of religion and good government in the late
elections, Again, in America, in every important town,
bands of Catholic young men are ever ready to repel
instantly any attack on their religion, and there is no
doubt that if such organisation and education of
Catholics had been in Trance and Portugal, we would
have been spared the late irreligious scenes which have
so disgraced these once Catholic lands. In Belfast
there is ample room for a mighty lay apostolate which
shall ever be on the watch against insidious or open
attack of our Faith, and it behoves all ‘especially to
guard against the dissemination of literature hostile to
the Catholic reigion. The Society of St. Vincent de
Paul can form walls of protection here; moreover, it
can go further, and circulate good healthy reading in
their respective parishes, therefore this Congress marks
a new era of prosperity and work for the Faith. Need-
less to say, that this weighty pronouncement found
immediate and cordial approval from the sturdy
Northmen, who will go forward, secure of victory, under
the wise leadership of the prudent and far-seeing
prelate of Down and Connor.
